segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
LOGAN KILPATRICK: Awesome.

Welcome to day two of I/O, first
I/O dialogue session coming

off tons of incredible
announcements yesterday,

led by most of the folks
who are on this stage.

So I'm super excited
for this conversation.
My name is Logan Kilpatrick.

I'm a PM inside of DeepMind
working on developer stuff,
working on Gemini.

So I see a bunch of
familiar faces from folks
who are hosting in the crowd.
So thanks for being here.

And I'm excited
to talk about all

of the AI moment, the
behind-the-scenes stories of all

the launches that we saw
today being made possible

again by the team inside of
DeepMind and across Google.

We'll kick off with
some super quick intros.

Koray is our CTO in DeepMind,
been leading DeepMind

for a long time now, one of
the leaders in the AI space
in artificial intelligence.

You're historically a research
scientist and engineer.

So you come from that
background, so excited
to get your perspective today.

Liz leads our Search
team inside of Google.

You've been at Google
for 22 years now,

helping shepherd Search into
the AI and agentic era, which
has been awesome to see.

You also led Maps
before as well.
Awesome.
And Josh, making I/O happen.
ELIZABETH REID: [LAUGHS]

LOGAN KILPATRICK: Josh, I think
of you as the emcee of I/O
almost.
JOSH WOODWARD: No.

LOGAN KILPATRICK: So I think we
need to codify that officially
next year.

But Josh, you lead the Gemini
app team and Google Labs.

And Google Labs is responsible
for a bunch of the product
launches that we saw yesterday.

So we'll see and we'll talk
more about that later today.

So I'm excited for
this conversation.

Thank you all three for
taking the time to chat.

Let's start off with this
thread around, Koray, for you,

Gemini being built to
be natively multimodal.

We hear this story a lot
about Gemini from the origin,

with Gemini 1.0, it was
natively multimodal.

It could take in images,
audio, video, et cetera.

And I was listening to a podcast
that you were on last night.

And you were saying that
this is fundamentally

because we believe that
the way to get to AGI
is by having a multimodal model.

Taking that at face
value, when you

think about the
opportunities for Google--

and we had some of these
that we announced yesterday

where we create bespoke
independent of Gemini models--

can you talk about where the
balance is between those models
and mainline Gemini?
KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: Yeah.
Thank you very much.

First of all, great to
be here with everyone,

welcome, and sharing the
stage with all of you folks.
What you said is right.

From the point of view of
building AGI and doing research

in that direction, of course,
that multimodality is important.

We've got to be able to get in
all the different perceptive
streams into the model.

And when we say multimodality,
sometimes we think about just
like video, audio, text.
But it's also more than that.

Actions are there,
they are important.

So we are thinking about
tool calls, function calls,

these kinds of things that
are natural for agents,

models to do, anything
that is in that spectrum.

The models should be able to
take them input and output.

And then about the
bespoke models,

building on this,
when you do research,

it's always important that
you explore something,
you explore an idea.

And that's always
critical to do that.

You need, in a way, that
sanitary environment

to push a particular
idea, figure out

what works, what
doesn't, and prove that.

But a big part of the
investment in the Gemini program

has been to bring things
in as quickly as possible.

And of course, it's a
challenge, because merging

a lot of different threads
in one model, a model that

is capable of doing coding
and text generation and image

generation and
everything in one place.

But you also start getting
the effect of the transfer
between those different fields.

So I think my canonical example
for this is vibe coding.

Vibe coding works
because you have

a model that understands the
world so that you can really

give a high-level description
of what you want to see,

rather than a technical
description of what
you want to see.

And you can even
do that with voice.

You can speak to the
model, say that, oh,

I want an application that
can do this and this and this
and that.

And by the way, here's
a little sketch.
And I want it to look like that.

And then the model
can generate that.

That capability, to me, is
where everything comes together.

And that's why we keep
doing that, right?

LOGAN KILPATRICK:
I have one quick

follow up just to rehash
part of the conversation we
were having back backstage.

One of the announcements
from yesterday

was this Diffusion
Gemini experiment.
KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: Yeah.

LOGAN KILPATRICK: And it's
truly a research experiment,

as our conversation was
going through yesterday.

How do you think about that
as an example of where we're

doing experiments versus where
the mainline capabilities are
going?

KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: That's a
really exciting experiment.

Because diffusion models
have been around for a while.

All the image and video
generation models that we have
are diffusion models.

And doing text generation
with diffusion,

I think it's something that
the researchers in AI field

have been experimenting
with for a while.

It's not that easy,
but it has advantages.

The reason we are pushing
it as an exciting direction

is it might have
its own advantages,

like looking at a large
chunk of output space.

And then to be able to, in
a way, go back in that space

and correct the
mistakes and such.

So for really complex
domains like coding,
this might be an advantage.

That's why we are
exploring that.
And it's an exciting avenue.
Now the job for us is--
it is exciting.

I think the demo
is very convincing.

And we know that there is
something we can get there.

But we have a bunch of steps
before we can comfortably

bring it into the main
Gemini and make it available.

That's what we are
working on right now.

LOGAN KILPATRICK: And
one more quick follow-up

to just help set the
context for this year's I/O

and also just for the broader
conversation around what's
happening in AI is around scale.

And we had this conversation
as a broken record.

We're always
talking about scale.

There's scale of
model, progress,
and the size of the models.

There's the
infrastructure scale.

How are you thinking about
the scaling from a DeepMind
perspective?

If you were making
an investment in,

here's how to bucket
out the scale advantage

that you want to try to create,
how are you thinking about that?

KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: Look, there's
a lot of discussion about scale
and what it means.

When we look at
model performance,

there are a bunch of
factors that go into that.

And even scale is correlated
with many of them.

But of course, I think
what is on top of mind

when we talk about
scale for everyone

is the scale of the
model, size of the model.

And the way I think about
this is there is something

that we know that we increase
the size of the model,
it does better.

That's a given, because you have
more computational performance
there.

Of course, if you think that is
the only way to improve things,

then you basically end up
suppressing all the creativity
that can come.

So at least I can
speak for Gemini,

what we try to do is have a very
broad spectrum of explorations
in many things.

So I try to think about
three main buckets.
One is data.
Data is really important.

Data is not just about
getting data into the model.

It's also about, there's
a lot of creativity
that you can put there.
How do you get that data?
How do you represent that data?

What do you learn from
different kinds of data streams?

What is the correlation between
those, especially when we
go to multimodality?

There's very important,
exciting research there.

And then there's the
model architecture itself,

the algorithm, the
optimization methods--
all those are very important.

And in each one of
these, actually there's
the scaling aspect.

And then the next thing that
you have is inference time.

When you think once
you have the model,
then it's the inference time.

So the techniques that
are in inference time
are multiplicative in a way.

Because you have a model
that has a certain capacity
at that point.

But you can train it to be a lot
more effective when it is being
used to reason for longer.

So there are many more areas,
but these three main buckets

are, I think, a good way to
think about it-- data, model,
inference time.

And scale is one
aspect of research

that you can do across all,
but there are many others.

And algorithmic improvements
are actually quite critical.

And a lot of the improvements
I think that are happening

are coming from a
mix of everything.

LOGAN KILPATRICK:
That's awesome.

So I think this scale thread
goes perfectly to Search, Liz.

And I think Search is such
a great example of where

you have these really,
really tough scale

problems at the size and
all the latency constraints.

I'm curious what Gemini is
enabling in search today

that, historically,
you wish 10 years ago,

we would have been able
to do, but we just now

have the technology
to actually make

this possible at the
scale actually of Google

Search, which is such
a challenging problem?

ELIZABETH REID: I
think one thing that

has been really
exciting is we started

with this mission of organizing
all the world's information.

But for a long time, your
ability to answer a question

was kind of limited based on
whether or not the answer to it
was all in one place.

And so that sort of
forced you, as a human,

if you had a multi-part
question, if you had five

constraints, to basically
take the question

and break it up into each
constraint and issue, like, OK,

you're going camping
with five kids and a dog,

and your in-laws, and
here's what you need.

Then you'd be like,
"camping with the dog,"

"camping with kids,"
"camping with--" right?

And so you would do all of this
work to break the question up,

because you would think that
maybe nobody in the world
wrote a web page just for you.

And one of the things we're
able to do with Gemini,

we call it the query
fan-out technique.

We talked about
an AI Mode, and we

use a simpler version of
this in AIO, in which you go

and you say, OK, instead of you,
the user, having to do this,
the model does this.

So then the model goes
and says, actually, these
are all the different pieces.

And it queries the web, and then
it pulls all the information
together.

And so that ability to say,
we can now answer a question

as long as the
information in its whole

is somewhere on the
web, as opposed to

in one spot on the web,
is really remarkable.

That lets you ask a lot
of harder questions,

but it also actually
lets you do something

that I think is less
obvious to folks in the US,

which is that the content
in various languages

and in various countries is just
a lot smaller than it is here
in the US and in English.

And so previously, your
access to information

was partly limited based
on the language you spoke.

And now with the
models, you can ask

the question in your
language, and we

can find the content
in another language

and we can transform it back
in a way you understand.

And then if you
want to continue,

you can go deeper on the web
using a tool like Translate

to access that
web page and that.

And so that sense that the
barriers to information

cease to be things
like language, I think,

is really incredible in
opening up new opportunities
for people around the world.

LOGAN KILPATRICK:
That's awesome.

Josh, we are having the
conversation this morning

about the future of I/O
and also just reflecting

on this year's I/O and how it's
become very clear that Gemini

is this thread that
brings all of Google

together across all of our
products and Search and YouTube

and for our enterprise
customers in Cloud

and everywhere
else in all the lab

stuff that your
team has shipped.

Can you give us the
high-level of where

you think, from a
Gemini app perspective,

the Gemini app plays
in there and what
the north star looks like?

JOSH WOODWARD: I think
with the Gemini app,

we've always been
interested-- and you

heard us talk about it
yesterday too-- in what would it

take to build a truly
universal AI assistant.

And that's been something,
even if you go all the way

back to the original
I/Os on stage,

when we introduced
the Google Assistant,

you'll find those
phrases are there.

And I think what's exciting
about this moment is the models

and the capabilities
are now at a point,

just like Koray
was saying and even

what Liz was saying,
where you can actually

take a real shot at
building something--

we talk about it on the team--
that's personal, proactive,
and powerful.

And when we think about
those three P's, we
want it to be personal.

As much info as you want
to give it-- it's up to you

how much you want to give
it-- but if you give it

a lot of your other
information across Google,
it can do useful stuff for you.
That's the personal bit.

I think the proactive one is
something I'm so excited about.

Because so much of AI
today is actually reactive.

And so you have to ask
something and then it answers.

But if it can
bring things to you

at just the right moment,
that I think really

changes the whole
user experience.

And then I think the
powerful part, a lot of you

hopefully saw some of this
stuff or you've gotten a chance
to play with Veo 3.

Have you had a chance to
try it in the Sandbox?
It's amazing.

And I think if you think
about video, imagery, coding,

all these things coming together
in an assistant that knows you,

that can anticipate
things, and you can give it

tasks to help do,
that's what we're
going to try to take a shot at.

And there's some of the
stuff that landed yesterday.

And we're rolling out
a whole bunch of stuff
over the next coming weeks.

LOGAN KILPATRICK: It's
been wonderful to see
that vision come to life.

And people are really
picking up on the three P's.

So we were also joking
backstage that we

need swag that talks
about the three P's,

because I think it's
going to keep coming up,

from a universal
assistant perspective.

Liz, I think there's also been
this broader thread about just

like what an AI-enabled
search looks like.

And I'm curious, yesterday
when the rollout for AI mode

started to all US users, it
feels like this huge step
forward for search.

And I'm curious just what
the behind-the-scenes
of actually making that happen?

And I have so much empathy
for you and the Search team.

Because I feel like Search
has this incredibly important

stewardship position
to play in the world

and can't just go and
break a bunch of things.

Because we really are the
front door for the internet.

So I'm curious how you
think about that and also

how the moment of rolling out
yesterday actually happened?

ELIZABETH REID: I think
there's a bunch of constraints,

but you can also
view the constraints

as space for creativity
and really think about it.

I think one of the things we've
seen with gen AI in Search

is both that it can
be extremely useful,
but also the bar is high.

People have a great experience
with a lot of things on Search.

And so if you're going
to bring in gen AI,

you can't be like,
aha, gen AI is cool,

I'm just going to
add it to Search.

It actually has to be, it's a
better experience for people.

And so we've rolled
this out through a bunch
of different ways.

One is just really
paying attention

to what really
matters to people.

So things like,
is it trustworthy?
How high accuracy is it?
And is it helpful?

Is it more helpful than
the rest of the page?
Latency was a big thing.

There are multiple times where
we wanted to roll out a bigger

model on a slice of
queries, and we didn't pick
the right slice of queries.

Because what happened was
it was a better response,

but the amount of additional
time it took to respond

was not worth the added
value to the user.

And there's not a
magic thing where

users are willing to wait 500
milliseconds all of the time.

It's like some things they
would spend 30 minutes doing.

And so they'll give
you 5 seconds to do it.

Because 30 minutes, 5 seconds,
it's a pretty good trade-off.

Others, they're like, uh-uh,
you're past 1.2 seconds,

I'm not going to
use you as much.

And that may sound
like an exaggeration,

but literally we would
see people's search

usage vary based on getting
the quality and speed trade-off
right.
So we had to work on them.

We were like, OK,
well, this model

is too big for it or we
don't have the right size,
but we can do it for math.
OK, let's get a better model.

And so we work closely
with Koray's team

on trying to figure
out how you get
the models that maximize that.

You also think about
latency in multiple ways.

Latency is partly this question
about, can you get started?
So people only read so fast.

So you don't have to have
the entire response available

before someone
does start to read.

You have to have confidence
that the first things you say
are good.

So you can't just
go and be like, I'm
going to start talking.
Oh, that was bad.
Let me start over.
And that really matters.

But you want to think about
that latency then of, OK, well,

what's the first
part I can give you?

And then I can give you
additional information
by the time you get there.
And then it feels fluid.

So there's all these little
subtleties in how you indicate.

Things like bolding, which
I don't think the world's

like, if you talk
to a normal person,

they're like, why
does bolding matter?

Bolding really matters
to people because bolding
helps with efficiency.

How quickly can
you skim and find
the relevant part of that thing?

And so there's a lot of both
core underlying technology.

But there's a lot of
product craft on top of it

to getting those little things
that really matter to people.

And so we're
constantly both trying

to push the boundaries on
the underlying foundations,

but also working to
get those little things

on the product that just make it
feel smooth and really helpful.

And then Search becomes more
of a delight and effortless
versus like it's a chore.

KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: To jump in
quickly, what Liz said is true.

All these models have
been great collaboration.

And we learn a lot
from that as well.

Because I think Search is the
greatest product in the world,

and it has the strictest
constraints in a way.

And what you said
is really true.
Constraints are creativity.

In an unconstrained
world, it's really hard

to choose why do you do one
thing over the other one.
So we use that a lot.

And then this is actually
our [INAUDIBLE] frontier

of trying to get higher quality,
faster, higher quality faster.

And that's basically
what leads to models

like Flash, Flash-Lite, and
more specialized things.

So this is a great
example of how

we try to push
technology forward,

getting constraints and feedback
from users and end products.

LOGAN KILPATRICK:
And, Liz, how do

you think about
that relationship

that Search has
with users in those

cases where they're
willing to only wait

one second versus cases
maybe where they'd
be willing to wait hours?

And you just launched Deep
Search inside of AI mode.

And that's the experience
of much longer-term tasks

that Search is
taking on for you.

So how have you
thought about this sort
of balance between those?

ELIZABETH REID:
So technically, we

announced Deep Search
is coming very soon.
LOGAN KILPATRICK: Coming soon.

ELIZABETH REID: But
not quite yet, in case
you're all looking for it.

But it is something that
we've had to pay attention to,

both from a query slice, but
also how do you empower people?

So AI Overview because
it's in main search

has the strictest
latency expectation.

But we have AI
mode when you want

to have the more advanced
thinking and reasoning.

But we still assume
that people, most

of the time when they're using
that, want it pretty fast.

And then it's like,
OK, you're going

to go buy a new appliance for
the first time in 10 years.

You would normally have
spent three hours on it.

You're clearly willing to wait a
few minutes for an expert level

report that gives
you great links.

I don't know how many
of you have kids.
I have three kids.

Camp planning, it's
kind of a mess.

They all have
different start times.

And I'm willing to wait
quite a few minutes if you're

going to come back with
an answer that saves me
hours and hours of work.

And so that is really
that question about,

can you give people
different options

so that they can really
choose how much time they're
trying to put in a task?

And can you constantly
be sensitive to what

is really the underlying task
the user is trying to do?

Because the query they come
to you is sometimes the task.

But oftentimes it's like a
proxy for the task, where they
did some amount of the work.

And so if you go back and you
step and say, what are you
really trying to do?

Then you can think about
new ways to solve it.

LOGAN KILPATRICK: Liz,
I love this example

that you give around just how
long is somebody willing to wait

to do something or
how much time would it
have taken them to do this.

I think about a lot
of tasks, for me

personally, where
they're like, you

should spend three hours
looking at appliances.

What actually happens is I just
go and find the second thing.

And I'm like, OK, this
looks pretty good.
I'm just going to do that.

And my partner always
pokes fun at me for that.

So it's also fascinating
to see these opportunities

where AI is going far and beyond
what humans would normally

have done for people who aren't
going to spend the time doing
that, which is just awesome.

ELIZABETH REID: I also
think that's a great example

in that there are
people who will

spend days and days double and
triple checking the toaster--
[LAUGHTER]

--and others who
apparently do not.

And so when you build a tool
that billions of people use,

you need to make it work
for both Koray and Logan.

And it's not a
one-size-fits-all.

And I think that's what's
interesting about Gemini working

on the universal
assistant being personal.

I think that's what
we're going to continue
to see in Search as well.

We're talking about
being more personal.

Personal isn't just, do you
have a better recommendation?

It'll probably be like, how
hard do you want to work?

How do you actually
want to learn?

Do you want to learn
by audio or visual?

Do you want to learn
on your way home?

Or do you want to do it while
you have five minutes here?
And how does that change?

And we just have to adapt
because it's important

that the tech
comes to the people

and not the people
come to the tech.

That's what had to happen
when the tech was limited.

But as much as possible, we
should meet people the way

that they want to engage,
not the way that we think
is the best for them to engage.

JOSH WOODWARD: And if I could
add one thing on top of that,

I think, to tie back to
one of the things Koray

said about Gemini being
natively multimodal.

I was telling this to
a friend the other day
who's not in Silicon Valley.

They were asking me, what's
one of your biggest surprises

over the last year working
on AI stuff every day?

And the first thing
that came to mind

was this idea that content
is now infinitely remixable.

And just like Liz
is saying, you can

have a set of things
that are documents

that turn into a podcast
or a set of things that

come in as images and
videos and everything else,

and it turns into a
multi-step action plan.

So I think this
idea-- especially

as there's a lot of builders,
I recognize a few of you

out here in the
audience-- as you're

thinking about your
applications and the problems

you're trying to solve
for your customers,

I would just try to take that
as an assumption that's already

mostly true today,
and just getting

better and better and better,
this idea that you can remix
content and weave it together.

Because that's
just been something

I've been really blown away how
each generation of the Gemini

models just keeps
getting better at that.

There's no end in
sight, from what we

can see, say, on the Gemini
side or the Google Labs side.

And it enables all kinds
of new experiences.

LOGAN KILPATRICK: On this
thread of new experiences,

when we landed Gemini 2.0,
one of the things that

was so exciting about this was
that the model had native tool
support.

And one of the native tools
that Gemini models are actually

fundamentally trained to
know when to use is Search.

And Koray, I think now we're
having this really interesting

moment where it's like
reasoning plus tools looks

a lot like this thing is just
kind of an agent out of the box
in a lot of ways.

I'm curious the balance, from
a model training perspective,

where we get the
model to use tools,

where it doesn't use tools, how
that interacts with reasoning?
Where's your head at?

KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: That's
a really important point.

I think, especially
the idea of tool calls,

function calls, that's
the next big frontier.

I think when you look around
the field, a lot of progress

has happened in the
last six months on that.

But I feel like we are just
scratching the surface.

When I think about ourselves,
a big part of our daily lives
is we use a lot of tools.

We build a lot of tools,
and then we use those tools.
We let others use those tools.

There's a world in
which the models should
create their own tools as well.

We are just actually
enabling the models
to use the tools that is around.
And we started with Search.

Of course, it's
the natural thing.

We are at Google,
we have Search.

It's amazing to be able to use
the knowledge, the freshness,

the information about
world that is in Search

and the model trying to figure
out when and how to use Search.

So there's a lot of
research that went into it.

But now I think it's
not perfect, of course.

But a lot of it was about, given
what the user conversation is,

how can we figure out a way to,
OK, is this the right time to go

and do a search and
get fresh information

or factual information
from the web,

rely on the search
technology that

has built all that information,
and then integrate that
into the response to the user?

And of course, we
see the benefits

of that in terms of factuality,
freshness of information.

And then we started more and
more generalizing from there.

But I think native
search integration
is really critical for Gemini.

I think it's an important
